texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane distillation (MD) is considered as a key technology for desalination applications. It shows indeed numerous advantages compared to reverse osmosis and other desalination processes (e.g. thermal driving force, no osmotic pressure effect on membrane fluxes). Nevertheless, this technology still presents some issues, most notably due to pore wetting effects. This study proposes the use of a thin, self-standing dense membrane in place of microporous materials as a solution to avoid wetting in MD for water desalination. In a first step, a membrane contactor model is developed based on mass and energy balances and the simulations are validated using experimental results obtained on hollow fiber modules with microporous membranes. A comparative performance analysis is then achieved between a porous and a dense membrane module. A parametric study on the influence of dense membrane thickness and water permeability shows that a two fold increase in water flux, without significant impact on energy efficiency, is potentially achievable with thin and permeable dense materials compared to microporous membranes. Guidelines for the design of high-performance dense membrane modules for MD are finally proposed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to provide the vapor pressure difference through the membrane, various configurations have been proposed. In direct contact membrane distillation (DCMD), cold water is circulated on the permeate side; the trans-membrane temperature gradient is responsible for the vapor pressure difference, and the vapor is re-condensed in the cold permeate. In the air gap (AGMD) configuration, the vapor is condensed on a cold plate placed in the permeate. In sweep gas (SGMD) membrane distillation, an inert gas is circulated in the permeate carrying out the water vapor; the vapor is later condensed outside the membrane module. Finally, for vacuum membrane distillation (VMD), vacuum is applied on the permeate side providing a high vapor pressure difference through the membrane. The water is recovered as vapor and then condensed outside the module.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Even if MD is a promising technology, various drawbacks emerge from the operating conditions and the membrane characteristics. Pore wetting, fouling, temperature and concentration polarization and scaling are the main issues that limit the application of membrane distillation in industry and its commercialization (Souhaimi and Matsuura, 2011). Different innovating processes and new materials have been proposed to address the troubles encountered in MD processes. For instance, carbon nanotubes generating Joule effect have been proposed in order to keep up the heat in the feed solution from inside the module, avoiding in this way temperature polarization (Boo and Elimelech, 2017; Dongare, 2017). Other nanomaterials, such as zeolites and graphene, have been also presented as potential high performance membranes in order to increase permeation flux and salt rejection (Daer et al., 2015; Balta et al., 2012).

Different innovating processes and new materials have been proposed to address the troubles encountered in MD processes. For instance, carbon nanotubes generating Joule effect have been proposed in order to keep up the heat in the feed solution from inside the module, avoiding in this way temperature polarization (Boo and Elimelech, 2017; Dongare, 2017). Other nanomaterials, such as zeolites and graphene, have been also presented as potential high performance membranes in order to increase permeation flux and salt rejection (Daer et al., 2015; Balta et al., 2012).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Among the aforementioned drawbacks, wetting is a major issue that can turn the operation into an inefficient process. Wettability occurs in two cases: first, when the minimum pressure needed to overcome the hydrophobic forces is reached (liquid entry pressure - LEP), and second, when there are salt incrustations or deposits on the surface of the membrane that decrease the membrane hydrophobicity. At this point, the liquid enters and wets the pores. A wetted pore causes a displacement of the vapor/liquid interface and salt is then able to cross the membrane with the liquid. The implications of wetting on MD performances differ depending on the kind of configuration used. In DCMD, the water flux increases when the pores are completely wetted, nevertheless the operation loses its purpose since the salt rejection drastically decreases. In VMD, as the membrane gets wetted, the salt enters the pores and form deposits on them blocking the passage of the vapor, thus decreasing the transfer rate.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In some studies carrying out long term membrane desalination operations for direct contact and vacuum membrane distillation (Gryta, 2005, 2000, 2002; Criscuoli and Carnevale, 2015), it was demonstrated that with a hydrophobic porous membrane there was no pore wetting when demineralized water was used. However, when tap water was used, the calcium carbonate present in the water precipitated on the membrane walls and caused a decrease of the flux. In this case, the membrane had to be washed with hydrochloric acid at 2-5 wt.% every 40 to 80 h to remove the CaCO 3 deposits and recover the efficiency of the module (Gryta, 2005; Schneider et al., 1988). The deposition of the crystals was proven using SEM microscopy before and after the DCMD operation (Karakulski and Gryta, 2005).

However, when tap water was used, the calcium carbonate present in the water precipitated on the membrane walls and caused a decrease of the flux. In this case, the membrane had to be washed with hydrochloric acid at 2-5 wt.% every 40 to 80 h to remove the CaCO 3 deposits and recover the efficiency of the module (Gryta, 2005; Schneider et al., 1988). The deposition of the crystals was proven using SEM microscopy before and after the DCMD operation (Karakulski and Gryta, 2005).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In another direct contact membrane distillation system used to concentrate waste water from heparin production, the solutes in the feed solution facilitated the fouling and therefore the wetting of the membrane, decreasing the flux by half in less than 60 h (Gryta, 2000). It has been demonstrated experimentally that parameters such as the porosity and pore diameter also change in time which can explain the variations of the water flux in time (Barbe et al., 2000).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A partial wetting of the membrane surface provokes a decrease in the water vapor diffusion path and thus an increment in the transferred flux through the membrane. Nevertheless, the wetting of the large pores at the surface quickly causes the wetting of the small pores, and the membrane ends up getting completely wet, decreasing to zero the efficacy of the process (Gryta, 2005).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the case of vacuum membrane distillation, wetting of the pores does not significantly affect the distillate flux, even though the purity of the permeate is affected by this phenomena. When complete membrane washing is applied after each test, the concentration of salt returns to the normal value, corresponding to deionized water. The passage of salt is thus a major issue in that case, because cleaning and turn-off the operation are needed. Membrane wetting can be explained by two steps: first, salt deposition in the pores and then salt particle entrained by vapor molecules (Criscuoli and Carnevale, 2015).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The presence of salts thus affects the membrane wettability; the fragments of membrane occupied by the salt crystals get easily wetted. A supersaturation of salt at the membrane surface due to the concentration and temperature polarization leads to an increase in the deposition rate; the entire pore volume occupied by the crystal is then filled up with water until wetting the membrane. It can be seen as a chain reaction; once the membrane is partially wetted, the crystal deposition continues in the following pores leading to a complete wetting (Gryta, 2002). For seawater applications, fouling of the surface by hydrophilic contaminants, such as deposits of silica particles, iron oxides or calcium carbonate not only block the pores, and therefore the permeate vapor channels, but also provide hydrophilic pathways for moisture intrusion.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Besides wetting, temperature and concentration polarization, phenomena in which the values of temperature and concentration at the boundary layer differ from those in the bulk solution, also provoke a decrease of the water flux. Part of the heat required for the evaporation can also be lost by heat conduction through the liquid and the membrane. Given that the vacuum imposed in the permeate creates an isolation compartment that minimizes the conductive heat loses, the configuration providing the higher thermal efficiency is VMD. This configuration also maximizes the water flux recovery because it provides the higher water partial pressure difference across the membrane. However, this configuration is also more propitious to develop wetting problems. The ideal condition for MD would be the use of a not wetted membrane (i.e. pores filled with vapor), guaranteeing a high water vapor flux, and at the same time a low thermal conductivity. These conditions are usually considered to maximize the MD vapor transfer. For these reasons, a VMD configuration has been selected in this study since it provides the best performances.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To address the key problem of pore wetting discussed above, we propose to explore the possibilities offered by a different kind of membrane. The porous hydrophobic membrane, classically proposed up to now, is replaced by a dense self-standing membrane. When a dense membrane is used in VMD, it could be considered that the operation is a pervaporation process. Nevertheless, the same separation principle is applied in the two cases: liquid separation takes place thanks to a difference in partial pressure across the membrane; the specificity of using dense self-standing membranes or composite membranes with a dense layer has simply to be taken into account in the calculation of the membrane mass transfer coefficient. The overall mass and heat transfer framework remains similar from the equation point of view. It could be stated that pervaporation separation is based on the difference in transport rate of the different species trough the membrane. The compounds of the liquid mixture can cross the membrane according to their own driving force and membrane permeability. In the case of desalination, salt does not vaporize and thus cannot cross the membrane; consequently, water vapor is the only transferred compound, similarly to what happens with a porous membrane for MD application (Fig. 1). Strictly speaking, the membrane is not selective to water vapor, but the non volatility of the salt species generates a selectivity, such as in an evaporation process. Taking this into account, desalination using a dense membrane can thus be considered as analogous to membrane distillation. Various publi- cations indeed refer to desalination with dense membranes as a membrane distillation operation instead of pervaporation (Criscuoli and Carnevale, 2015; Schneider et al., 1988; Karakulski and Gryta, 2005). A similar position has been adopted hereafter.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Interestingly, the use of dense membrane materials for MD applications was proposed as early as 1968 by Bodell who patented a system in which silicon rubber is used for saline water distillation (Bodell, 1968). Nevertheless, this alternative remained essentially unexplored for a long time. The use of dense skin composite and self-standing dense materials for MD has been more recently investigated in order to prevent wetting. In a recent study, Bell (2016) compares a porous PVDF membrane with a composite membrane of PVDF coated with a 2.4 l mlayer of a polyelectrolyte polymer in a DCMD unit. The coated membrane proved to be most stable in time and to avoid wetting. However, it presented a 15% reduction of the permeate rate compared to the porous membrane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In another study (Khayet and Matsuura, 2004), pervaporation is compared to VMD using PVDF flat sheet prepared from the same material but using different methods to produce porous and dense membranes. The thickness of the dense membranes ranged between 38 to 110 l m. The fluxes were lower for the dense membrane, even though, for the thinnest membrane, the flux was only 1.5 times lower. The dense membranes used in this study were in any case too thick to compete with the porous materials. However, this study points out the interest of dense membranes for distillation. Xu et al. (2006) also used dense membranes for MD. This time, a thin silicone rubber hollow fiber with a thickness of 40 l m was used in ASMD and VMD systems. The silicon based membrane showed a water rate comparable to the microporous membrane and leakage of the feed water to the permeate was prevented.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The use of a dense layer has thus proven to be very effective to avoid wetting. Similar conclusions have been obtained for other membrane processes where wetting is present and limiting (Makhloufi et al., 2014; Belaissaoui, 2016). These studies have shown that composite membranes can compete with high performance microporous membranes achieving mass fluxes equally high (Nguyen et al., 2011). Nevertheless, a coated membrane requires the implementation of difficult methods to superimpose a thin film on the porous material. The materials used should be compatible and sometimes problems such as filling of the pores with the dense material can occur. A dense composite material is also subject to wetting by capillary condensation of the vapor in the porous support.

texts.orig: A dense membrane also avoids the deposition of salts in the membrane surface and then pore blocking, giving a 100% rejection and better stability over time. The aforementioned publications on dense-self standing membranes suggest interesting impacts on the membrane performances. Despite the low fluxes found with dense membranes, promising results in terms of flux stability are reported. An analysis on the conditions and characteristics of a perfect dense self-standing membrane for MD is thus critically needed. This study intends to address this target, thanks to a parametric simulation strategy.

texts.orig: The performance analysis of a MD process basically requires to define membrane materials properties, module geometry and operating process conditions. In terms of membrane material, three major types, shown in Table1 can be proposed for MD applications. Most of the studies are based on hydrophobic microporous membranes, while few publications report the use of dense skin composite membranes for wetting protection applications. Dense selfstanding materials, shown on the last column of the table, will be investigated hereafter. More specifically, the possibility to simultaneously achieve perfect wetting protection thanks to a dense structure and similar MD performances (i.e. transmembrane water flux and energy efficiency) will be investigated.

texts.orig: As observed from Eq. (1), the permeability coefficient depends on the properties of the membrane material: porosity e , membrane thickness d , tortuosity s and pore diameter. Generally, the pore diameter should be 0.3 l m or smaller in order to avoid wetting. Moreover, the optimal membrane thickness is found between 20 and 200 l m. The smaller the thickness, the higher the flux even though a thin membrane could cause higher heat losses by conduction. The porosity should be the greatest possible, e > 75 % ; guarantying a high insulation, and thus a high thermal efficiency. A low tortuosity is recommended (1.1 to 1.2). When a high porosity is attained, the tortuosity is expected to be low enough. The adequate LEP to avoid wetting is around 2 : 5 bar ; this value depends on the material properties and the hydrophobic character of the membrane (Pangarkar et al., 2014; Eykens et al., 2016). A contact angle of about 120  is recommended to guarantee the hydrophobicity of the membrane.

texts.orig: In contrast to porous membranes, the fluids on each side of the membrane are in equilibrium with the material, which implies a discontinuity in the concentration profiles at the interfaces. This equilibrium is governed by the solubility of these compounds in the polymer. The driving force of transport through a dense membrane is the concentration gradient. In the case of MD, this concentration gradient results in a difference in vapor pressures on both sides of the membrane. Transport is ensured by maintaining a lower permeate vapor pressure than that of the feed side.

texts.orig: The mass transfer resistance by a self-supporting dense membrane is always much higher than that of a dry microporous membrane of the same thickness. Due to the absence of porosity, the effective diffusion coefficients of the transported molecules are indeed several orders of magnitude lower than those observed in a microporous membrane. Consequently, the thickness of the dense material should be brought to a very low value in order to compete with the performances of dry microporous membranes.

texts.orig: Interestingly, recent studies have revealed the feasibility of producing such thin, high permeable membranes. In a study (Soyekwo, 2017); a cellulosic composite membrane with a thickness of 64 to 111 nm was synthesized. A smooth, dense, ultrathin PEI barrier was observed on the surface of this cellulosic membrane with an estimated thickness of around 10 nm. In another study, several nanometric thin films were fabricated using block copolymers, PEO and PBT. The thickness of the membranes varied between 80 and 500 nm. These membranes were applied to the CO 2 absorption, finding a higher permeability than commercial membranes (Yave et al., 2010).

texts.orig: Self-standing nanofilms of polysulfones doped with sulfonate polysulfones were also prepared via solvent evaporation obtaining a self-standing film of 46 nm thickness (Yuan et al., 2017). In another study (Karan et al., 2015); 10 nm thick polyamide membranes were produced using controlled interfacial polymerization; the nanofilms were sufficiently rigid to withstand pressurized filtration. Fluxes are more than two orders of magnitude larger than commercial membranes, under similar operating conditions.

texts.orig: Heat transfer is different in a dense and in a porous membrane. In a porous membrane, the heat flux through the liquid qF is equal to the heat needed for evaporation plus the conductive heat lost in the membrane and permeate qT . The later is very low due to the insulating effect of the porous membrane and the vacuum in the permeate. The temperature drop takes place mainly in the membrane and there is no loss on the vacuum side, where the temperature is considered constant and equal to Tm ; P . The evaporation takes place at the feed-membrane interface, at a temperature T ¼ Tm ; F .

In a porous membrane, the heat flux through the liquid qF is equal to the heat needed for evaporation plus the conductive heat lost in the membrane and permeate qT . The later is very low due to the insulating effect of the porous membrane and the vacuum in the permeate. The temperature drop takes place mainly in the membrane and there is no loss on the vacuum side, where the temperature is considered constant and equal to Tm ; P . The evaporation takes place at the feed-membrane interface, at a temperature T ¼ Tm ; F .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the case of a dense membrane, the heat conduction through the membrane is higher leading to a slight temperature drop through the membrane. Evaporation occurs on the permeate side at temperature T ¼ Tm ; P . The main temperature drop occurs at the permeate. In this case, the heat transfer from the feed to the membrane is equal to the heat needed for the evaporation plus the heat lost by conduction in the permeate.
texts.text: In the case of a dense membrane, the heat conduction through the membrane is higher leading to a slight temperature drop through the membrane. Evaporation occurs on the permeate side at temperature T ¼ Tm ; P . The main temperature drop occurs at the permeate. In this case, the heat transfer from the feed to the membrane is equal to the heat needed for the evaporation plus the heat lost by conduction in the permeate.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A hollow fiber module is simulated in order to compare under the same operating conditions (feed water flow, inlet temperature, water salinity and vacuum pressure) the VMD performances of microporous and dense selfstanding membranes.
texts.text: A hollow fiber module is simulated in order to compare under the same operating conditions (feed water flow, inlet temperature, water salinity and vacuum pressure) the VMD performances of microporous and dense selfstanding membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A porous membrane was chosen as the base case for comparison. The characteristics of the membrane were chosen so that the performances are maximized: a high value of water permeance as well as a large porosity are selected. Furthermore, the pores of the base case porous membrane are assumed to be completely dry, so that the flux is the highest possible and a very good thermal efficiency is obtained. The water permeance value is set at 5 : 56 -10 � 7 kg m � 2 Pa � 1 s � 1 for a 450 l m thick membrane and at 1 : 67 -10 � 6 kg m � 2 Pa � 1 s � 1 for a thickness of 150 l m. It can be seen in Table 2 that these conditions correspond to the most permeable microporous membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the dense membrane module simulations, water permeability varied from 10,000 to 100,000 Barrer in order to cover and compare a large variety of materials and to find out which of those materials is the most relevant for VMD. These values are among those usually found in the literature for water transport through dense membranes (Table. 3). The thickness of the membrane varies from 50 nm to 5 l m. The membrane thickness is tested for very low values, which are clearly exploratory at this stage. It is indeed often considered that the lowest thickness for dense selfstanding hollow fibers is in the range of 5 l m. It is important to stress however that recent studies (Yuan et al., 2017; Karan et al., 2015) have shown the feasibility to prepare very thin membranes at laboratory scale, exhibiting good mechanical and chemical properties. The production of very thin self-standing dense hollow fiber may thus become possible in a near future.

texts.orig: It must be taken into account that the dense membranes were compared to highly efficient porous membranes. The porous membrane with a thickness of 450 l m gave an average flux of 30 kg m � 2 h � 1 while the one of 150 l m, considered as more suitable for desalination, gave a flux of 60 kg m � 2 h � 1 . texts.label: text
texts.orig: From Fig. 7a, we can conclude that a dense membrane can reach high fluxes and can compete with porous membranes, as soon as the thickness is 1 l mor lower. Under this value of thickness, even the less water permeable membranes of around 20,000 Barrer generate water fluxes above the baseline case. It is interesting to note that using a thin and highly permeable membrane, the flux obtained can be twice the flux provided by a high performance porous membrane. The dense selfstanding membrane concept is thus very attractive because it combines an increased productivity and wetting protection (which ensures flux stability and does not require membrane cleaning steps).

texts.orig: 6.2. Thermal efficiency
texts.text: 6.2. Thermal efficiency

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Besides productivity expressed through water flux, module thermal efficiency is also of major importance. Thermal efficiency measures how well heat is used inside the membrane module. It can be expected that dense materials generate larger thermal losses, due to the larger thermal conductivity of dense polymers compared to dry microporous membranes. Energy efficiency is classically calculated by dividing the total flux required for evaporating the water by the overall energy lost by the feed between the inlet and outlet of the module. Fig. 7b shows the evolution of the energy efficiency as a function of the thickness and water permeability of the membrane.

texts.orig: In the case of a dense membrane, the thermal conductivity corresponds to that of the polymeric material and ranges between 0 : 11 � 0 : 22 W m � 2 K � 1 ; higher than the value for a porous membrane. The heat is thus more easily transferred through a dense membrane by conduction. Besides, as the dense membranes considered are very thin, the heat transfer coefficient is even higher. Nevertheless, the presence of vapor at vacuum conditions in the permeate compartment strongly limits the heat losses. As a consequence, the temperature at the membrane wall on the permeate side is almost equal to the temperature at the membrane wall in the feed side Tm ; P  Tm ; F . The heat lost through the dense membrane are minimal, and the thermal efficiency is close to 100 % Fig. 7b. The same type of behavior is observed in pervaporation where the heat lost by conduction through the membrane and vacuum is very low (Favre, 2003).

texts.orig: Thermal efficiency enables to evaluate how heat is distributed inside the membrane module (evaporation, loses by conduction, convection). Another kind of efficiency can be also calculated: energy efficiency or second low efficiency. This efficiency will take into account all the operations needed for the functioning of the desalination unit (pumps and heater) giving a wider view of the heat use in the unit. The energy efficiency is however more useful when comparing different kinds of MD (VMD, AGMD, DCMD, SGMD) or when comparing to different methods for desalination (reverse osmosis, flash distillation). In our case, as comparing different membrane materials in the same MD unit, no big difference is expected for the energy efficiency. The work done by Deshmukh and Elimelech (2017) proposes a deeper analysis on this subject.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 7. Conclusions and perspectives
texts.text: 7. Conclusions and perspectives

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane distillation is a technology showing very attractive performances for water desalination or solute concentration applications (Souhaimi and Matsuura, 2011; Pangarkar et al., 2014; Lokare et al., 2017; Joo and Kwak, 2016). Vacuum membrane distillation (VMD) is often preferred in terms of operating mode because it ensures both a high productivity (maximal driving force due to vacuum pumping) and maximal thermal efficiency (i.e. insulation provided by vacuum on the downstream side). Microporous hydrophobic membrane materials are classically used in order to achieve a large water flux and efficient thermal insulation. Nevertheless, membrane wetting and fouling is often considered as the major limitation of the technology in that case, leading to decreased performances over time. The use of a dense skin coated on a porous membrane support has been recently shown to provide a very effective protection against wetting and fouling (Bell, 2016). Dense skin composite materials remain however subject to capillary condensation effects in the support. Moreover, the production of dense skin composite materials can be complex and may show limited mechanical resistance.
texts.text: Membrane distillation is a technology showing very attractive performances for water desalination or solute concentration applications (Souhaimi and Matsuura, 2011; Pangarkar et al., 2014; Lokare et al., 2017; Joo and Kwak, 2016). Vacuum membrane distillation (VMD) is often preferred in terms of operating mode because it ensures both a high productivity (maximal driving force due to vacuum pumping) and maximal thermal efficiency (i.e. insulation provided by vacuum on the downstream side). Microporous hydrophobic membrane materials are classically used in order to achieve a large water flux and efficient thermal insulation. Nevertheless, membrane wetting and fouling is often considered as the major limitation of the technology in that case, leading to decreased performances over time. The use of a dense skin coated on a porous membrane support has been recently shown to provide a very effective protection against wetting and fouling (Bell, 2016). Dense skin composite materials remain however subject to capillary condensation effects in the support. Moreover, the production of dense skin composite materials can be complex and may show limited mechanical resistance.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This study evaluates a new type of membrane material, namely dense self-standing polymeric hollow fibers for VMD. Even though, thin (i.e. below 5 l m ) selfstanding fibers are not commercially available yet, recent studies have reported very thin self-standing membranes at laboratory scale (down to 7 nm (Karan et al., 2015) sufficiently rigid to withstand pressure filtration under 10 bar , thus offering attractive possibilities. Other nanometric self-standing membranes with a thickness between 46 nm and 2.6 l m have been also produced at laboratory scale proving the feasibility of thin membranes preparation. These membranes also present good mechanical resistance and strength, which are needed for vacuum membrane distillation. For instance, the ultrathin membranes produced by Yuan et al. (2017) can withstand a hydraulic pressure up to 7 : 7 kPa.

texts.orig: It is obvious however that dense self-standing membranes synthesis for industrial applications remains a challenging task. New production techniques, which might be difficult to implement are to be developed. The fabrication procedure and conditions should be precisely controlled in order to avoid crumple of the nanofilms or structural defects. Their later implementation in a flat sheet or hollow fiber module remains a complicated task to solve for future applications development. The possibilities offered by this new family of membranes are clearly of interest and the studies mentioned before show promising prospects for a new generation of MD modules.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: More specifically, a systematic parametric study has been achieved in order to evaluate the possibility to compete with microporous membranes in terms of productivity (water flux) and energy efficiency. The ideal membrane distillation material has been indeed analyzed by Findley in 1967 (Findley, 1967) and should combine maximal mass transfer performances together with perfect thermal insulation properties. These conditions have been recently explored through a quantitative study which proposes a set of guidelines for the best MD membrane materials (Eykens et al., 2016; Cabassud and Wirth, 2003). A trade-off has to be faced because a very large porosity (80-90%) is needed for mass transfer and thermal insulation properties but this is hardly compatible with maximal mechanical resistance (tensile strength). Moreover, wetting remains a potential problem due to the porous structure and sensitivity to pressure (necessity to stay below the Liquid Entry Pressure).
texts.text: More specifically, a systematic parametric study has been achieved in order to evaluate the possibility to compete with microporous membranes in terms of productivity (water flux) and energy efficiency. The ideal membrane distillation material has been indeed analyzed by Findley in 1967 (Findley, 1967) and should combine maximal mass transfer performances together with perfect thermal insulation properties. These conditions have been recently explored through a quantitative study which proposes a set of guidelines for the best MD membrane materials (Eykens et al., 2016; Cabassud and Wirth, 2003). A trade-off has to be faced because a very large porosity (80-90%) is needed for mass transfer and thermal insulation properties but this is hardly compatible with maximal mechanical resistance (tensile strength). Moreover, wetting remains a potential problem due to the porous structure and sensitivity to pressure (necessity to stay below the Liquid Entry Pressure).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This study has shown that the mass transfer and energy efficiency performances of microporous membranes can be attained by dense self-standing polymeric hollow fibers. Interestingly, water productivity could be potentially increased by a factor of two, with a negligible impact on thermal efficiency, while ensuring wetting and fouling protection due to the non-porous, low surface energy structure. As shown in the previous section, some existing dense materials, permeable enough to water Tables 3 and 6 , are able to compete with a porous membrane, if the thickness can be brought to small values. Commercial membranes based on dense polymers with a high permeability to water, are already applied for gas drying applications (Favre, 2010); the challenge is to reduce the thickness of these membranes conserving their mechanical strength. Various studies have proved that the use dense layers or self-standing dense membranes efficiently avoid wetting and achieve 100% rejection of salt and particles. These performances have to evaluated and corroborated based on thin selfstanding membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As a conclusion, implementing a dense membrane in vacuum membrane distillation, as long as it is sufficiently thin and permeable, offers promising perspectives compared to current performances obtained with porous membranes. This study has shown the potentialities of dense membranes, as soon as a thickness around 1 l mor less is achievable. The results from this study open new investigation tracks for researchers working on membrane materials and membrane desalination process for improved desalination technologies.
